cuisine: Turkish / Middle Eastern (served in Singapore context)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fried fish dish; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of fried fish in a Singapore Turkish-style restaurant is approximately 250 g, equivalent to 2–3 pieces of seasoned and deep-fried fish fillet or whole fish portions.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried (SR Legacy ID 15264) and Fish, tilapia, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy ID 15262), cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for fried fish dishes
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values (energy 232 kcal, protein 15.2 g, fat 15.2 g, carbohydrate 8.8 g, sodium 300 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for breaded and fried fish (energy ~228–240 kcal/100g, protein 14–16 g, fat 13–16 g, carbohydrate 8–10 g). Values adopted from input with USDA cross-validation. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 32 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 72 mg, saturated fat 3.2 g, trans fat 0.2 g, vitamin C 4.0 mg) are plausible and consistent with USDA fried fish profiles. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100g based on USDA SR Legacy fried fish data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in the input; set to -1 with documentation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried fish with mixed flour coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Turkish-style fried fish. GI of 55 is estimated based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered/breaded fried fish products with mixed flour coatings (GI range 50–60). While 55 is numerically in the medium range, the University of Sydney GI Database classifies values 55 or less as 'low' for practical purposes when considering mixed meals and the impact of protein and fiber. Carbohydrate per serving = 8.8 g/100g × 250 g serving = 22 g. GL = (55 × 22) / 100 = 12.1. The moderate protein content (15.2 g/100g) and dietary fibre (1.2 g/100g) from the flour coating further moderate the glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100g (750 mg per 250 g serving) is in the borderline-high range for hypertensive individuals; deep-frying adds caloric and fat load
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive individuals should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces, and prefer pan-fried over deep-fried preparation to reduce total fat and caloric density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8.8 g/100g) with estimated GI of 55 yields a medium glycaemic load per serving; high fat from deep-frying may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should prefer pan-fried or baked preparation to reduce fat absorption, limit portion to 150–200 g, avoid high-sugar dipping sauces, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the overall glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.2 g/100g with saturated fat 3.2 g/100g and trans fat 0.2 g/100g; deep-frying increases oxidised lipid content and caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fried fish to occasional consumption, request pan-fried or baked preparation to reduce saturated and trans fat intake, and avoid reused frying oils which elevate oxidised lipid exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) which directly exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations entirely and request pan-fried or baked fish to eliminate AGE formation; turmeric in the marinade provides beneficial anti-inflammatory curcumin and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish is a popular dish served in Turkish and Lebanese restaurants in Singapore, featuring a whole fish or fish fillet that is seasoned with spices such as turmeric, chili powder, cumin, and garlic, coated in flour or gram flour (besan), and deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crispy. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of a crunchy exterior and moist, flaky interior. In the Singapore context, it is commonly served as part of a mixed plate alongside rice, salads, or flatbread. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 232 kcal, 15.2 g protein, 15.2 g fat (3.2 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 8.8 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The moderate sodium level (300 mg/100g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and the deep-frying method increases caloric density and fat absorption. The estimated glycaemic index of 55 (low) reflects the mixed flour coating and is moderated by the protein and fibre content. Clinically, pan-fried or baked preparation is preferred for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore Turkish restaurant contexts.

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for breaded and fried fish. No exact approved-source match for Turkish-style fried fish in Singapore; values adopted after USDA cross-validation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence set to medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Turkish-style fried fish not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the input source and are not available for this specific Turkish-style fried fish preparation in USDA SR Legacy or HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100g based on USDA SR Legacy fried fish data (SR Legacy ID 15264). Two portions in the input both marked is_default=true; the 250 g plate portion is used as the canonical serving size for GL calculation. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but the primary nutrient evidence is cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved source. Input aliases were empty; full multilingual alias set constructed per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for Singapore-served dishes.
